risk tolerance level
Our risk tolerance defines the level of risk we are willing to take to achieve our strategic objectives and
category.
The Board of Directors is responsible for approving the Group’s risk exposure level and conducting regular reviews to help ensure that the
The level is still appropriate.
Risk tolerance levels are applied at the overall enterprise level and through a number of quantitative and qualitative risk management mechanisms
systems and activities, and are formally stated in the risk tolerance statement.
The Group will consider the following when setting its risk tolerance level:
Consistent with the group's strategy, purpose, values, external risk environment, reputation and customer needs;
Comply with applicable laws, regulations and regulatory priorities;
a forward-looking view of future risk exposure;
the adequacy of available capital, liquidity and balance sheet leverage to absorb risk;
The size and capabilities of risk management personnel;
The functionality, scale and resilience of available risk management systems;
The effectiveness of the appropriate control environment in mitigating risk; and
Disclosed internal and external commitments.

Quality level definition
– “Stable”: Risk projects that have sufficient ability to fulfill financial commitments, have a minimal or very low probability of default, and/or have very low expected loss levels.
– “Good”: Risk projects that require closer monitoring, have good ability to fulfill financial commitments, and have low default risk.
– “Satisfactory”: risk projects that require closer monitoring, have average to average ability to fulfill financial commitments, and have medium default risk.
– “Below standard”: risk items that require varying degrees of special monitoring and where default risk is of greater concern.
– ‘Credit Impaired’: Risk items that have been assessed in the manner described in note 1.2(j) to the financial statements.

The table below sets out an overview of the Group's credit risk by grade and industry, and the associated expected credit loss coverage. Financial assets recorded at each level have the following characteristics:
Level 1: This type of financial assets is not impaired, and the credit risk has not increased significantly, and a 12-month expected credit loss provision has been recognized.
Level 2. The credit risk of such financial assets has increased significantly after the first recognition and accounting, and expected credit losses within the period have been recognized.
Level 3. These financial assets have objective evidence of impairment and are therefore considered to be in default or credit-impaired, with expected credit losses recognized within the period.

Financial assets purchased or undertaken with impaired credit: Financial assets purchased or undertaken at a substantial discount are deemed to reflect the credit losses that have occurred, of which expected credit losses within the period have been recognized.

Consistent upward trend
Compared with the core situation, economic activity in the consistent upward situation is stronger in the short term and then gradually returns to
The expected level of the long-term trend. The unemployment rate in the consistent upward scenario is lower than that in the core scenario, and asset prices are
higher. Inflation accelerated moderately, driven by increased investment and rising consumer spending.
This scenario is consistent with several key upside risk themes, including the rollback of some tariff measures, regulatory
The relaxation of regulations, the improvement of Sino-US relations, and the easing of the geopolitical situation.

Downside
The downside scenario simulates the situation when multiple major risk themes intensify and actually occur. The model shows that
Economic shocks have led to reduced consumption and investment, as well as falling commodity prices. Most markets
Inflation and interest rates are both below core-scenario levels, but the situation in the United States and Mexico is different.
The additional tariffs and other countermeasures are expected to lead to a general increase in import prices.
Key downside risks include:
Protectionist policies have increased, reducing investment, complicating international supply chains and hampering
trade relations;
Given high valuations (especially in the tech sector), asset prices have corrected sharply, eroding wealth
effect, ultimately leading to an increase in credit risk;
The scope of conflicts in the Middle East has expanded and continued, coupled with the Russia-Ukraine war, weakening market confidence
and investment; and
Disagreement between China and the United States continues, affecting economic confidence, global commodity trade, and key technology supply chains.

Climate Risks Working Group on Climate-related Financial Disclosures
Our climate risk management approach defines the two main contributors to climate risk:
Physical risks: As extreme weather events (such as hurricanes and floods) become more frequent and severe,
or risks arising from gradual changes in weather patterns over time or rising sea levels; and
Transition risks, risks that arise in the process of moving towards a net-zero economy, including government policies and legislation
changes in law, technology, market demand, as well as changes in the expectations of relevant groups, adoption or non-adoption
The reputational impact of actions.
We continue to identify thematic issues related to climate risk that may be reflected in reputational, regulatory
Compliance and litigation risks arise in the form of: greenwashing risk, which arises from intentional or unintentional
Continue to make inaccurate, unclear, misleading or unsubstantiated claims to relevant groups.
Net zero adjustment risk used to be regarded as a thematic issue, but now it is a new risk type "sustainable".
"Continuous development execution risk" will be replaced and managed.

policy

The principal business activities of a group of related counterparties are identified by the industry in which the majority of revenue is generated within the group. We adhere to high standards of corporate governance. The Group has comprehensive policies and procedures in place to help ensure the proper and overall management of the Group's governance through effective and appropriate supervision and control.
Items to be dealt with by the Board of Directors are set out in
For its terms of reference, please visit

The Board of Directors collectively assumes overall responsibility for the Group's success in achieving its long-term goals and delivering sustainable value to shareholders. The board of directors is headed by the group chairman and is responsible for the following matters:
Review and approve the group's strategies and goals, and monitor whether the group's purpose, strategies and values are consistent with the ideal culture and standards;
Establish risk tolerance levels for the group and monitor the group's risk profile;
Approve and oversee various capital and financial resource plans to achieve strategic objectives, including major transactions;
Review and approve the Group’s technology and environmental, social and governance strategies;
Review whether the communication mechanism with employees and other relevant groups is effective;
Approving the appointment of directors and board positions, as well as the remuneration of independent non-executive directors;
Review and approve changes to the Group’s overall corporate governance arrangements; and
Provide corporate leadership to the group to enable risks to be assessed and managed within a prudent and effective governance structure.

The Board of Directors has delegated the power to supervise certain matters to its sub-committees, and each sub-committee of the Board of Directors is chaired by a non-executive director. Committees under the board of directors regularly submit work reports and suggestions to the board of directors

Make suggestions. Only the group chairman and non-executive directors can be members of the committees of the board of directors. For details about committee members, please see Directors in the “Board of Directors” chapter on pages 187 to 190

Board positions and responsibilities

The positions of Group Chairman and Group Chief Executive Officer are held by different individuals. The Group Chairman is responsible for the leadership of the Board of Directors, while the Group Chief Executive Officer is responsible for the day-to-day management of the business. The responsibilities of the two are clearly divided.
work. The table below summarizes each director's position and related responsibilities. For details, see https://www.hsbc.com/who-we-are/our-people/board-of-directors/board-
Position
Responsibilities
– Effectively lead the Board and promote the highest standards of corporate governance practices.
– Lead the board of directors to conduct rigorous strategic oversight and set the board’s agenda, culture and values.
– Lead the board of directors in questioning management’s ideas and suggestions and promote open and constructive debate among directors.
– Maintain internal and external relationships with key stakeholder groups and communicate investor perspectives to the Board.
– Arrange for regular monitoring and evaluation, including hiring external agencies to assist in evaluating the performance of the board of directors, its committees and individual directors.
– Lead the succession planning of the Board of Directors and its committees to ensure that appointees have diverse cultures, skills and experiences.
– Lead and guide the group to achieve its goals and strategies, in line with the ideal culture and values set by the Board of Directors.
– Lead the Group Operations Committee in accordance with the authority granted by the Board of Directors and shoulder the responsibilities of daily leadership and management of the Group.
– Maintain good relationships with key internal and external relevant groups (including group chairman, board of directors, customers, regulators, government and investors).
– Be accountable for the Group’s compliance with applicable laws, codes, rules and regulations, good market practice and HSBC’s own standards, values and policies.
– Assist the Group Chief Executive to formulate and implement group strategies, and provide advice on annual budgets and long-term strategies and financial resource plans.

– Lead the work of the Finance Department and be responsible for ensuring the effective preparation of financial and regulatory reports, including the effectiveness of relevant procedures and control measures, to ensure that the financial control structure is robust and appropriate.
– Maintain relationships with key stakeholder groups, including shareholders.
Senior Independent Director
– Assist the Group Chairman in the performance of his duties and act as a liaison with other non-executive Directors when necessary.
– Lead the non-executive directors to supervise the work of the group chairman and ensure a clear division of labor between the group chairman and the group chief executive.
– Listen to shareholders’ concerns that cannot be resolved through normal channels.
– Provide advice on formulating group strategies.
– Challenge and monitor management’s performance in achieving established corporate goals.
– Assist in assessing and monitoring corporate culture.
– Maintain internal and external relationships with the Group’s key stakeholders.
– Maintain strict and consistent governance practices within the Board and throughout the Group.
– Assist the Group Chairman in ensuring the effective functioning of the Board and its committees, as well as communication between senior management and non-executive directors.
– Assist non-executive directors to receive mentoring and professional development.
– Provide advice and support to the Board of Directors and management to ensure effective end-to-end governance and sound decision-making throughout the Group.

Employee compensation structure

The key features of the remuneration structure set out in the table below apply to the entire Group (excluding executive directors), subject to local legislation. The remuneration structure of the overall staff is similar to that of executive directors, including

It includes two elements: fixed remuneration and floating remuneration, and applies deferral, lock-up, deduction and withdrawal policies to floating remuneration. A summary of the executive directors' remuneration policy is set out on page 218.
Compensation components and targets
Applied to group employees
Salaries and allowances
– We provide market competitive remuneration that is consistent with the position, skills and experience requirements.
– In addition to basic salary, fixed remuneration may also include fixed remuneration allowances, cash-in-lieu pensions, and other cash allowances based on local market practices.
– Fixed remuneration may vary based on individual position, responsibility or grade, cost of living in country/region, individual skills, abilities and experience.
Benefits and Pensions
– Benefits may include, but are not limited to, pensions, medical insurance, life insurance and health assessments provided in accordance with local market practices.
– All employees may be considered for discretionary variable pay awards. Individual rewards are determined based on performance targets set at the beginning of the year.
– Variable remuneration accounts for a larger proportion of the overall remuneration of more senior employees to strengthen the relationship between total remuneration and performance.
– Unless local regulations provide otherwise, the maximum limit for employees’ variable remuneration is ten times the fixed remuneration.
– Awards are generally paid in cash and shares. For persons exposed to significant risks, at least 50% of the award must be in shares and/or (if required by law) in asset management
It will be issued in the form of management fund-linked units.

– Members of the Group Operating Committee and other senior personnel of the Group are also eligible to be considered for mid-term incentives, but must meet three-year forward-looking performance measurement indicators.

The relationship between risk, performance and rewards

Our remuneration policy promotes sound and effective risk management to support the achievement of business objectives and implementation of relevant strategies. The following sets out the key features of the Group’s structure that help us comply with local
Balancing risk, performance and rewards under laws and regulations:
architectural elements
Application

The Group's variable remuneration is expected to reflect the Group's performance and is determined by a number of financial and non-financial factors. We adopt a countercyclical capital allocation method and set structural payout ratio ranges for different levels of profitability.

range, and has a lower limit and an upper limit. When performance improves, payout ratios generally decrease to avoid procyclical effects. The lower limit is set because we recognize that even in tough times,

Staying competitive remains important. The upper limit is set because under better performance conditions, floating remuneration does not necessarily have to continue to increase, thereby limiting improper behavior in order to improve financial performance.
The main quantitative and qualitative performance and risk indicators used to assess performance include:
The financial performance of the Group and business units, taking into account environmental factors and capital requirements that drive performance;
Current and future risks, taking into account performance relative to risk tolerance levels, financial resource planning and global conduct performance; and
Fines, penalties and provisions for customer compensation are automatically included in the Commission's definition of profits used in determining variable compensation funds.

If the group is unable to pay dividends to shareholders due to capital adequacy ratio and other reasons, the group may determine that the performance of the year is weak. The above-mentioned measurement indicators can be used as the basis for this decision during the year, and some or even all of the employees may be deducted.
Variable compensation, including share awards that have not actually been vested.

The Group Remuneration Committee will make recommendations based on the comprehensive assessment results of risk management performance, and the committee may, at its discretion, increase or decrease the amount of funds based on these recommendations.
personal performance

Individual performance is assessed against clear and relevant financial and non-financial objectives. The objectives of the Group Operating Committee members are to effectively manage corporate risks, ensure regulatory compliance and prevent financial crime risks

responsibility, and managing financial risk. The above goals are independently evaluated by the Risk Management and Compliance Department. Employees and the Group CEO will be informed of the risk management and compliance ratings and evaluation results as a year-end review

of reference materials. Direct reports and other senior executives of members of the Group Operating Committee will be assessed based on the risks, regulatory and financial crime prevention objectives identified in their responsibilities. all other members
Employees are assigned mandatory risk management and compliance objectives.

Performance reviews for all employees include behavioral assessments (where permitted by local legislation), as well as a comprehensive assessment of progress towards meeting targets and whether behaviors are consistent with HSBC’s values to ensure that performance assessments are
It’s not just about the results, it’s also about the way things are done.
Monitor department employees
Group policy prompts each supervisor to report to their respective infrastructure department. Salary decisions for senior infrastructure positions are made by global infrastructure executives.

The performance and rewards of personnel in each control department, including risk management and compliance employees, are evaluated based on their balanced scoring records against job-specific objectives.
The performance of the business units these personnel are responsible for supporting will not affect the determination of their remuneration.
We carefully compare remuneration with market and internal benchmarks to ensure remuneration is set at appropriate levels.
The committee is responsible for approving the remuneration of the Group Director of Risk Management and Compliance and the Head of Group Audit.
Variable compensation adjustment
and good rewards
Conduct

Variable compensation awards may be adjusted up or down to reflect good or poor behavior in compliance with the Code of Conduct. The following situations will trigger a downward adjustment, including:
Bad conduct, including conduct that damages HSBC’s reputation;
Involving an event that resulted in a significant operating loss, or an event that has caused or is likely to cause serious harm to HSBC; and
Failure to comply with conduct consistent with HSBC’s values and other mandatory requirements or policies.
Positive behaviors can be rewarded through the group's global recognition platform "At Our Best" or by increasing floating compensation rewards.
Haircut provisions may be applied to deferred awards granted in prior years but not actually vested (up to 100% of the award). Applicable situations include:
Bad conduct, including conduct that damages the reputation of the business;
Past performance has been significantly worse than originally reported;
restate, correct or amend any financial statements; and
Improper or inadequate risk management.

Withdrawal provisions apply to awards actually awarded or paid to persons subject to significant risk for a period of seven years. If, at the end of the seven-year period, the internal/regulatory investigation continues and the employee

If a member holds a senior management position designated by the UK Prudential Regulation Authority and the Financial Conduct Authority, the term can be extended to ten years. Withdrawal provisions also apply to persons with non-significant risks. Circumstances in which withdrawal provisions apply
Engage in conduct that causes significant losses to HSBC or be held responsible for such conduct;
Failure to meet appropriate standards and appropriate conduct;
There is reasonable evidence of misconduct or gross failure that would or should justify immediate termination of the employment contract; and
In terms of the Group's risk management standards, policies and procedures, HSBC or its business units have made significant lapses in risk management.

For awards that have been granted to a designated executive officer as defined by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC"), if the financial statements are required to be reconstructed due to a material violation of any financial reporting requirements of the U.S. securities laws,
Accounting data are presented, and the withdrawal clause also applies to awards that have been actually awarded or paid to these executive officers for a period of three years.
sales incentives

Identify the major
risk personnel

We identify individuals who are persons exposed to significant risks based on qualitative and quantitative criteria set out in the Prudential Regulation Authority and Financial Conduct Authority remuneration rules. HSBC’s identification procedures are based on
List the main principles:
Identify those with significant risk at group, HSBC Bank UK plc (consolidated) and HSBC UK levels.
Persons with significant risks are also identified at the level of other separately regulated entities in accordance with regulatory requirements.

When identifying people who are exposed to significant risks, HSBC considers the employee's role within its organizational management structure. The order of consideration is the business and infrastructure departments the employee is responsible for first, followed by the location where the employee works.
area.

HSBC also identifies other persons who are exposed to significant risks based on its internal standards, including certain positions and levels that would not be identified as persons exposed to significant risks under the remuneration rules.

Basis of preparation and major accounting policies
1.1 Compilation basis
(a) Compliance with IFRS

HSBC's consolidated financial statements and HSBC Holdings' separate financial statements comply with international accounting standards and the provisions of the Companies Act 2006 adopted by the United Kingdom, and have applied regulations applicable in accordance with the European Union (EC)

International Financial Reporting Standards No. 1606/2002. These financial statements are also in accordance with the International Financial Reporting Standards ("IFRS Accounting Standards") promulgated by the International Accounting Standards Board, including the International Financial Reporting Standards.

Interpretations issued by the Reporting Standards Interpretations Committee) have been prepared because there are no differences from IFRS accounting standards applicable during the period presented. There are no plans to take effect during the year ending December 31, 2025 that have not been formally approved.
The adopted standards affect these consolidated and separate financial statements.
Adopt IFRS accounting standards for the year ended December 31, 2025
There are no new standards, amendments to standards or interpretations that have an impact on these financial statements. Accounting policies are consistently applied.
Differences between IFRS accounting standards and Hong Kong Financial Reporting Standards

There are no material differences between IFRS accounting standards and Hong Kong Financial Reporting Standards in terms of the standards applicable to HSBC. Therefore, even if financial statements are prepared in accordance with Hong Kong Financial Reporting Standards, they are not

There will be no significant differences. The "Notes to the Financial Statements" together with the "Report of the Board of Directors" cover all information required to be disclosed under IFRS accounting standards and "Hong Kong Financial Reporting Standards".
(c) Future development of accounting treatment methods
Minor revisions to IFRS accounting standards

The International Accounting Standards Board ("IASB") has issued certain minor amendments to IFRS accounting standards that are effective from January 1, 2026. HSBC expects that the adoption of these amendments will have an impact on HSBC’s consolidated financial statements.
and HSBC Holdings’ standalone financial statements will have minimal impact.
Other amendments and new IFRS accounting standards
Amendments to IFRS 9 Financial Instruments and IFRS 7 Financial Instruments: Disclosures

In May 2024, the IASB issued amendments to IFRS 9 "Financial Instruments" and IFRS 7 "Financial Instruments: Disclosures", which will be effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after January 1, 2026. The amendment

In addition to providing guidance on the time at which certain financial liabilities can be deemed to have been settled when using electronic payment systems, it also provides further elaboration on the classification of certain financial assets that contain modifiable contracts.

Contractual terms on the timing or amount of cash flows, including financial assets arising from environmental, social and governance-related contingencies and certain non-recourse characteristics. The Group does not anticipate that such revisions will occur
any significant impact.
IFRS 18 "Presentation and Disclosure of Financial Statements"

In April 2024, the IASB issued IFRS 18 "Presentation and Disclosure of Financial Statements", which will be effective for annual reporting periods beginning on or after January 1, 2027. The new accounting standards are intended to provide financial statements

Provide users with more transparent and comparable information on an entity's performance. This standard will replace IAS 1 "Presentation of Financial Statements" but retains many requirements of IFRS accounting standards. In addition, the guidelines also propose three
Establish new regulations covering the structure of the income statement, performance measures defined by management, and the integration and classification of financial information.

Although IFRS 18 does not modify the recognition standards or measurement basis, it may have an impact on the information presented in the financial statements, especially the income statement, and the impact on the cash flow statement is smaller. HSBC
We are currently assessing the relevant impacts and ensuring that sufficient information is collected to prepare for the implementation of the amendments.
(d) Foreign currency

HSBC's consolidated financial statements are presented in U.S. dollars because the U.S. dollar and various U.S. dollar-linked currencies are the primary currency areas in which HSBC conducts transactions and funds its operations. The U.S. dollar is also owned by HSBC Holdings

The functional currency is because the U.S. dollar and various U.S. dollar-linked currencies are the most important currencies closely related to the relevant transactions, events and conditions of HSBC Holdings' subsidiaries, and at the same time, their financing activities generate huge
Some funds are also denominated in these currencies.

Transactions in foreign currencies are recorded at the exchange rate prevailing on the date of the transaction. Assets and liabilities denominated in foreign currencies are translated at the exchange rate on the settlement date, except for non-monetary assets and liabilities measured at historical cost using the initial exchange rate.

Exchange rate conversion on the day of transaction. Each exchange difference is recognized in the income statement, unless otherwise specified. For example, the exchange portion of non-monetary items' gains and losses will depend on where the gains and losses of the relevant items are presented.
It is recognized in the income statement or other comprehensive income.

Except for subsidiaries operating in hyperinflationary economies, in the consolidated financial statements, the assets and liabilities of branches, subsidiaries, joint ventures and associates whose functional currency is not the United States dollar are calculated at the exchange rate on the balance sheet date.

Translated into the Group's presentation currency, their results are translated into US dollars using the average exchange rate during the reporting period. The resulting exchange differences are recognized in other comprehensive income. Selling overseas business
Subsequently, exchange differences previously recognized in other comprehensive income were reclassified to the income statement.
(e) Presentation of information
Some of the disclosures required by IFRS accounting standards have been included in the section marked ("Audited") in the 2025 Annual Report and Accounts, as follows:
Information disclosed on the nature and extent of risks in policies and financial instruments is set out in "Risk Review" on pages 97 to 185.
"Own Funds Information Disclosure" is set out in "Risk Review" on page 159.

In addition, HSBC complies with the UK Financial Disclosure Code. The UK Financial Disclosure Code aims to improve the quality and comparability of disclosures by UK banks. It sets out five disclosures agreed in 2010.

Disclosure principles and supporting guidelines. In accordance with the principles of the UK Financial Disclosure Code, HSBC will evaluate the good practice recommendations issued from time to time by relevant regulatory agencies and standards-setting bodies, and will also evaluate these guidelines.
are applicable and relevant to the group, and will increase the level of disclosure where appropriate.

The preparation of financial information requires the use of estimates and judgments regarding future conditions. Given that there are inherent uncertainties and a high degree of subjectivity involved in recognizing or measuring items (see “Critical Estimates and Judgments” in Section 1.2 below,

"), the results for the next fiscal year may differ from the conditions based on which management made its estimates, which may cause significant deviations in management's estimates and judgments with respect to these financial statements. Management

The selected HSBC accounting policies (including key estimates and judgments) reflect the importance of the items to which the policies apply and the high degree of uncertainty involved in the judgments and estimates involved.

Management has reviewed the impact of climate-related risks on HSBC’s financial position and performance. Although the impact of climate change constitutes a source of uncertainty, as of December 31, 2025, management believes that

In the short term, physical risks, transition risks and other climate-related risks will not significantly affect key judgments and estimates. Specifically, management has reviewed the impact of climate-related risks on the judgments involved in calculating value in use.
Determine and estimate possible known and observable impacts.
(g) Going concern

The financial statements have been prepared on a going concern basis as the directors are satisfied that the Group and the parent company have the resources to continue the business for the foreseeable future. In making this assessment, each director has considered relevant current and future
Extensive information on conditions, including forecasts of future profitability, cash flows, capital requirements and capital resources.

These considerations include various stressful situations reflecting the uncertainty of the macroeconomic environment: continued supply chain disruptions, uncertain inflation, rapid changes in interest rates, the war in Russia and Ukraine, and the Middle East, Venezuela or other

the impact of further conflict or military action in other regions, uncertainties surrounding the commercial real estate industry in Hong Kong and Mainland China, intensifying strategic competition between the United States and China, and ongoing and potential cross-border investment and trade restrictions.
the potential impact of changes in regulations, tariff rates, and other primary and emerging risks, including climate change, and the associated impact on profitability, capital and liquidity.

Interests in associates and joint arrangements

A joint arrangement is an investment in which HSBC and one or more parties have joint control. Depending on HSBC's rights and obligations, the joint arrangement may be classified as a joint operation or a joint venture.

(b) Impairment of goodwill and other non-financial assets
goodwill
During impairment testing, goodwill is allocated to each cash-generating unit and tested at the lowest level monitored by internal management.
Impairment testing is performed at least annually or when there is an indication of impairment by comparing the recoverable amount of the cash-generating unit with its carrying amount.

Goodwill is included in the sale-for-profit business combination if it is a cash-generating unit to which goodwill has been allocated or is a business within the cash-generating unit. Amount of goodwill included in business portfolio for sale
It is measured based on the relative value of the business sold and the cash-generating unit portion retained.
Other non-financial assets

Software under development is tested for impairment at least annually. Other non-financial assets are property, machinery and equipment, intangible assets (excluding goodwill) and right-of-use assets. These assets appear at the individual asset level

When there is an indication of impairment, an impairment test is conducted at the asset level, or for assets with no recoverable amount at the individual asset level, an impairment test is conducted at the cash-generating unit level. In addition, if impairment occurs at the cash generating unit level
If there are any signs, impairment testing will also be conducted at this level.

Impairment testing compares the carrying amount of a non-financial asset or cash-generating unit to its recoverable amount, which is the higher of fair value less costs of disposal or value in use. The carrying amount of a cash-generating unit includes its assets

and the carrying amount of liabilities, including non-financial assets directly attributable to the cash-generating unit and non-financial assets allocated to the cash-generating unit on a reasonable and consistent basis. Non-financial funds that cannot be allocated to individual cash-generating units

Products are tested for impairment according to appropriate groupings of cash-generating units. The recoverable amount of a cash-generating unit is the fair value of the cash-generating unit less costs of disposal (as determined by an independent qualified valuer (if relevant)) and the price used
The higher value (calculated from appropriate data) (see Note 21).

When the recoverable amount of a cash-generating unit is less than its carrying amount, an impairment loss is recorded if the impairment can be allocated proportionally to the non-financial asset by reducing the carrying amount to the higher of the individually recoverable amount or zero.

Impairment losses recognized for non-financial assets in prior periods are reversed when there are changes in the estimates used to determine the recoverable amount. The impairment losses to be reversed shall be based on the fact that the book value of the non-financial assets is not higher than that of the previous period.

Policy summary

Multiple individual policies that are managed together and bear similar risks are identified as a policy portfolio. Policies administered together usually belong to the same product portfolio and have similar characteristics (such as similar pricing frameworks or

Product Management is similar) and issued by the same legal entity. If the policy is subject to more than one risk, the primary risk of the policy will be used to assess whether the policy has similar risks. per policy group

The syndicate is further broken down by the expected profitability of the policy. The policy portfolio will be divided based on its profitability into: (i) policies that are subject to onerous conditions when first recognized; (ii) policies that are unlikely to subsequently become conditional when first recognized

onerous policies; and (iii) other policies. The profitability policy portfolio is then divided by issuance date, with most policies issued by the Group after the transition date being grouped by quarter. For multi-currency policies
Portfolio, the Group treats the policy portfolio as if it were denominated in a single currency.

Policy liabilities are measured based on the policy portfolio divided upon initial recognition, which includes fulfillment cash flows and contract service revenue representing unearned profits. The Group’s accounting policy is to update accounting policies on a year-to-date basis.

Best estimate of future cash flows

(If the scale of the Group's own experience data is not sufficient to be considered reliable data) determine a series of scenarios and assumptions to forecast these amounts in an unbiased manner.
Adjustments for the time value of money and financial risks associated with future cash flows

The Group adjusts its estimates of future cash flows to reflect the time value of money (i.e. discount) and financial risks to arrive at the expected present value. The Group generally uses stochastic simulation techniques to estimate the options and guarantees attached.
Guaranteed product.

The Group uses a bottom-up approach to determine the discount rate used for a specific group of expected future cash flows. The relevant discount rate is calculated as the sum of the risk-free rate of return and the illiquid funds premium. The risk-free rate of return is

Determined based on observable market data and the relevant market is considered to be deep, liquid and transparent. When relevant data cannot be obtained, management will determine the appropriate risk-free rate of return. illiquid
The funding premium reflects the liquid nature of the underlying policy.
Risk adjustments for non-financial risks
Risk adjustment reflects the compensation required for the uncertainty in the amount and timing of future cash flows arising from assuming non-financial risks.

The Group does not disaggregate the risk-adjusted changes between insurance services results (including insurance revenue and insurance service expenses) and insurance finance income or expenses. All changes are included in insurance services results.
econometric model
Most of the policies issued by the Group use the variable fee method measurement model, which is mandatory when the following eligibility criteria are met at the time of entry:
The contract terms stipulate that policyholders jointly participate in relevant projects as part of a clearly specified package;

The Group expects to pay policyholders a significant portion of the fair value return on the relevant items. The Group believes that the significant portion represents a significant portion of the returns; and

A significant proportion of any changes in the Group's expected payments to policyholders will be due to changes in the fair value of the underlying items. The Group believes that a large proportion is weighted by the present value probabilities of all situations
A considerable proportion of the variation in the mean.

For certain contracts measured under the variable fee method, we use the alternative comprehensive income option. The other comprehensive income option applies to related items held by the group that are not at fair value through profit or loss.

Head. Under this option, only the amount that matches the income or expense recognized in the profit and loss account will be included in the financing income or expenses of the relevant policy, thereby excluding accounting mismatches. Issued during the period

The remaining financing income or expense amount of the policy is recognized in other comprehensive income. In addition, we also use risk mitigation options on a number of economic offsets for instruments that meet certain requirements.
The remaining policies issued and reinsurance contracts held are accounted for using the general measurement model.
Contract service income and protection units

Contractual service income represents unearned profits, so even if the policy portfolio is profitable, no income or expense will be incurred on initial recognition. Contractual service revenue is reflected in each subsequent reporting period in relation to future services.

Adjustments are made for changes in related fulfillment cash flows (such as changes in non-economic assumptions, including mortality and morbidity rates). When a portfolio of onerous policies is first recognized or when the portfolio subsequently becomes onerous
, the loss will be immediately recognized in insurance service expenses.

For policy portfolios measured using the variable fee method, contract service income will be adjusted based on changes in the group's share of relevant items, as well as changes in economic experience and economic assumptions. However, root

Under the risk reduction option of the variable fee method contract, changes in fulfillment cash flows and changes in the Group's share of fair value returns on related projects (mitigated by instruments) will not be adjusted in contract service income.
It is not consolidated but is recognized in the profit and loss account. Risk reduction instruments are primarily reinsurance contracts held.

As for the contract portfolio using the general measurement model, contract service revenue will not be adjusted in response to changes in economic experience and economic assumptions, but will be recognized in the profit and loss account when changes occur.

Contractual service revenue will be recognized in insurance revenue on a progressive basis based on the coverage units of the contract portfolio to reflect the policy services provided. The insurance unit is determined based on the number of benefits and the expected coverage period of the contract.

(o) Non-current assets and business portfolios held for sale

HSBC classifies a relevant non-current asset or business portfolio (including assets and liabilities) as held for sale if the carrying amount will be recovered primarily through sale rather than continuing use. To be classified as

To be held for sale, the relevant non-current assets or business portfolio must be available for immediate sale under the current conditions, and must be carried out on the normal and customary terms for the sale of such assets (or sale of business portfolio),

and the sale is highly feasible. For a sale that is highly feasible, there must be an appropriate level of management, a plan to sell the asset (or business portfolio for sale), and an active process to find the sale.

Buyer and completes the program. In addition, these assets (or business portfolios for sale) must be actively marketed at prices that are reasonable based on current fair value. In addition, the sale should be expected from the date of classification
A sale that qualifies as completed within one year and the actions required to complete the plan should indicate that the plan is unlikely to be significantly revised or withdrawn.

Assets and business portfolios held for sale are measured at their carrying amount or fair value less costs to sell (whichever is lower), except for assets and liabilities that do not fall within the measurement requirements of IFRS 5. If non-current capital

If the carrying value of an asset (or a business combination for sale) is higher than its fair value less costs of disposal, then any initial or subsequent write-down of the relevant asset or business combination for sale to fair value less costs of sale will result in an impairment loss.

Will confirm. Any such impairment losses are first allocated against the measurement of non-current assets within the scope of IFRS 5, i.e. first by reducing the carrying amount of any goodwill allocated to the relevant business portfolio for sale,

The carrying value of any goodwill allocated to other non-current assets in the portfolio is then deducted in proportion to the carrying value of each asset in the sale-purpose business portfolio. Thereafter, any non-current measurement that exceeds the scope of IFRS 5
An impairment loss on the asset's carrying amount is recognized for the total asset value of the sale-for-sale business portfolio.

The amount will be recognized as surplus only if it can be recovered or may be refunded through various plans in the future. In assessing whether the surplus is recoverable, HSBC has considered its current receipt of future refunds or reductions in future contributions.
rights, as well as the rights of third parties such as trustees.
master plan

The main plan consists of a defined benefit component and a defined contribution component. In 2015, the defined benefit portion stopped accruing future benefits, and employees’ defined benefits that had accrued before the date of cessation of accrual will be maintained while they are still employed by

HSBC continues to link its salary. The scheme is overseen by an independent corporate trustee, who has fiduciary responsibility for the operation of the scheme. Plan assets are held separately from group assets.

The investment strategy of the plan is to hold assets mainly bonds, with the rest being diversified investments, including some interest rate swaps, inflation swaps and life swaps to reduce interest rate risk and inflation risk respectively.
risk and reduce the impact of extended life expectancy.

The master scheme is subject to the statutory funding target provisions of the UK Pensions Act 2004, which requires funding to be at least a technical level (an actuarial estimate of the assets required to provide the benefits established in the scheme).
estimated). If a funding valuation is carried out and a deficit is discovered, the employer and trustee must agree to a deficit recovery plan.

The most recent funding valuation of the scheme was carried out by Towers Watson Limited on 31 December 2022 using the projected unit credit cost method. On that date, the market value of the scheme’s assets was £23.9 billion

(US$28.8 billion), which is £3.7 billion (US$4.4 billion) higher than the planned ongoing liability value, with a funding level of 118%. The figures include £3 billion ($3.6 billion) of defined contribution assets.

The main difference between the assumptions used in the funding valuation of defined benefit liabilities and those used in IAS 19 is that the funding valuation assumptions for discount rates, inflation rates and life expectancies include prudential elements. Fund estimate

The value is used to determine the amount of cash contributions that the group needs to make for the relevant pension plan. It is necessarily different from IAS 19 accounting earnings, which is an accounting rule for employee benefits and is presented in the group's financial statements.
's balance sheet. The next round of funding valuations will be conducted in 2026, with an effective date of December 31, 2025.

Actuaries also assess the value of the liability in the event that the plan terminates and the insurance company is required to underwrite all future pension payments. The amount is generally larger than the ongoing requirements mentioned above because the insurance company

The company will use more prudent assumptions for reserve purposes and take into account clearly planned future administrative expenditure reserves. Calculated based on this method, as of December 31, 2022, the amount of assets required is estimated to be 21.3 billion British pounds
pounds ($25.7 billion).

The trust deed allows HSBC UK to receive a refund of the remaining assets after the plan ends if there are no longer any beneficiaries. When assessing whether earnings are recoverable, HSBC UK has considered its future

The right to refund, and the rights of third parties such as trustees. On this basis, any net surplus on the HSBC UK portion of the scheme is recognized in the HSBC UK financial statements and the group financial statements.

The group's profits are taxed at different rates, with the applicable rate depending on the country in which the profits are generated. The main applicable tax rates in 2025 include Hong Kong (16.5%), the United States (21%) and the United Kingdom
(25%). If the Group's profits were taxed at the statutory rate of the country in which the profits were generated, the tax rate for the year would be 21.2% (2024: 21.4%).

The effective tax rate for this year is 22.7%, which is higher than last year (2024: 22.6%). The effective tax rate for the year increased by 1.2% due to the diluted losses and non-deductible impairment of the Group's investment in Bank of Communications.

Unrecognized changes in deferred tax increased by 1.0% (mainly related to French tax losses) and increased by 1.0% due to the impact of provisions for penalties and legal settlement expenses (no tax benefits were recorded). Mainly composed of

As the tax returns for the previous year were finalized in India and Hong Kong, we made adjustments to the previous figures, resulting in a 0.3% reduction in the effective tax rate for the year. The effective tax rate in 2024 is due to the sale of HSBC Canada assets

Decreased 3.6% due to non-taxable gains, increased 4.8% due to non-deductible losses arising from the sale of HSBC Argentina, increased due to unrecognized changes in deferred tax (mainly related to French tax losses)
an increase of 0.7%, and an increase of 0.7% due to the group's "second pillar" global minimum tax expense.

The UK has adopted the "Second Pillar" global minimum tax model rules of the OECD Base Erosion and Profit Shifting (BEPS) Inclusive Framework, effective from January 1, 2024. Several jurisdictions have adopted class

Similar rules and local minimum tax systems will take effect from January 1, 2025, while certain jurisdictions such as Bermuda have introduced new or revised corporate income tax systems that will take effect from that date.
The changes will result in an increase in local overseas tax liabilities and a decrease in UK top-up tax liabilities in 2025.

results, and the results of transactions between companies and business units. All such transactions were conducted on an arm's length basis. Segment assets, liabilities, income and expenses are measured in accordance with the Group's accounting policies.

Pay more attention to information that is considered more relevant and reliable. Considerations include observability of prices, comparability of instruments, consistency of data sources, accuracy of relevant data and timing of pricing
room.

As for the fair value determined by the valuation model, its control structure includes the logic, data, model projection results and adjustments used by independent logistics departments to develop or verify the model. Valuation models are subject to a due diligence process

The order can only be adopted and will be continuously calibrated based on external market data. Fair value adjustments are applied when no other factors are incorporated into the primary product valuation model.

Most of the financial instruments measured at fair value are derived from capital markets and securities services businesses. The fair value governance structure of the capital markets and securities services business includes its finance department and various valuation committees. wealth

The Finance Department is responsible for formulating procedures for regulating valuations and ensuring that fair values comply with relevant accounting standards. The fair value is reviewed by the Valuation Committee, whose members include the independent backend department.
Financial liabilities measured at fair value

In certain circumstances, HSBC will record its own debt issued at fair value, which is determined based on quoted prices in active markets for the specific instrument. If there is a lack of market quotation, it will

Use valuation methods to estimate the value of issued own debt. The input data used in the valuation will be based on the quoted price of the instrument in an inactive market, or through the price of a similar instrument in an active market.

Quotes are compared and estimated. In both cases, the fair value will include the impact of the credit spread applicable to HSBC's liabilities. Changes in the fair value of debt securities issued due to the Group’s own credit spreads

Movement is calculated by obtaining an externally verifiable price for each security at each performance reporting date or by using the credit spread on similar securities of the same issuer. Then, the group will

Using the discounted cash flow method, each security is valued using an appropriate market discount curve. The difference between the valuations is due to the Group's own credit spreads. This method is used consistently for all securities.

The structured notes issued and certain other hybrid instruments are presented as financial liabilities designated at fair value. The credit spreads applied to these instruments are derived from the interest rates used by HSBC when issuing structured notes.

fair value hierarchy
The fair value of financial assets and liabilities is determined based on the following hierarchy:

Level 1 - Valuation method using market quotations. The relevant financial instruments have quoted prices on active markets in which HSBC participates for identical instruments on the measurement date.

Level 2 – Valuation methods using observable data. The relevant financial instrument has a quotation for a similar instrument in an active market or an identical or similar instrument has a quotation for an inactive market, and uses all
All significant data are observable and the financial instruments are valued using models.
Level 3 – Use valuation methods that involve significant unobservable data. The financial instruments are valued using valuation methods that involve one or more significant unobservable data.

Fair value is estimated using a valuation model that incorporates a range of assumptions. Such assumptions may include: estimated values reflecting over-the-counter trading activity and provided by third-party brokers; forward-looking discounted cash flow models

Interbank deposits and customer accounts

The fair value of demand deposits approximates their carrying value. The fair value of longer-term deposits is estimated using the discounted cash flow method and is calculated based on the current interest rate on deposits with similar remaining maturities.

The fair values of debt securities issued and subordinated liabilities are determined based on quoted market prices (if any) at the settlement date, or by reference to quoted market prices for similar instruments.

Uses of Derivatives
For details on the use of derivatives, please see "Market Risk" on page 169.
Trading Derivatives

Most of HSBC's derivatives transactions involve sales and trading activities. Sales activities include designing and promoting derivatives products to customers, allowing them to assume, transfer, amend or reduce current or expected

period risk. Trading activities include market making and risk management activities. Market making activities involve quoting prices to other market participants (providing bid and ask prices) and earning income from price differences and trading volume.

Risk management activities are carried out to manage the risks arising from customer transactions, with the main purpose of maintaining customer margin. Other derivatives classified as held for trading include derivatives that are not eligible for hedging purposes.

Derivatives for hedging accounting purposes

HSBC applies hedge accounting to manage the following risks: interest rate and foreign exchange risk. For details on how these risks arise and how the Group manages these risks, please see “Risk Review”.
Hedging risk components

When HSBC conducts fair value hedging or cash flow hedging, it designates a portion of the cash flows of a financial instrument or a group of financial instruments in relation to a specific interest rate risk or exchange risk component. Relevant designations

Risks and components may be specified in the terms of the contract or may be individually identifiable components of a financial instrument that can be measured reliably. In addition to the second phase of the transition to interest rate benchmark reform, HSBC designated

In addition to using the alternative benchmark rate as a hedging risk that was not separately identified at the time of initial designation (provided that HSBC reasonably expects that it can meet the requirements within 24 months from the date of initial designation), the risk-free rate

Or the benchmark interest rate is generally considered to be both individually identifiable and reliably measured. These designated risk components may represent a substantial portion of the changes in the overall fair value or cash flows of the hedged item.

HSBC uses the net investment hedging method to hedge the structural exchange risk of the net investment in overseas operations (including subsidiaries and branches whose functional currencies are different from those of the parent company). When conducting forward foreign exchange
When a contract is hedged, the spot exchange rate component of exchange risk is designated as the hedged risk with respect to a certain net asset amount.

Ineffective hedging may result from basis risks, including, but not limited to, discount rates used to calculate the fair value of derivatives, hedging using non-zero fair value instruments, and name differences between the hedging item and the hedging instrument.

The value-in-use calculation method consists of two main components. The first part is management's best estimate of Bank of Communications' earnings. Forecast earnings growth in the short to medium term continues to be lower than in the recent past (within the last five years)

The actual growth reflects the recent impact of macroeconomic, policy and industry factors in mainland China. Since the management intends to retain this investment in the long term, profits in the short and medium term will be calculated using the long-term growth rate.

Extrapolate to an infinite period to arrive at the final value, which constitutes most of the use value. The second part is to maintain capital requirements, that is, management makes a forecast of the profits that need to be withheld in order to

This enables Bank of Communications to comply with capital requirements during the forecast period, which means that when management estimates the future profits available to ordinary shareholders, it first deducts maintenance capital requirements. Reasons for provision to maintain capital requirements reflected

Revised capital requirements resulting from revisions to the assumed ratio of risk-weighted assets to total assets. The calculation method for maintaining capital requirement provisions mainly takes into account asset growth valuation, risk-weighted assets to total assets

value ratio and expected capital requirements. If changes in these major input values ​​lead to an increase in the provision for maintenance capital requirements, the value in use will decrease. In addition, management also considers other qualitative factors to
Ensure that the input data used in calculating value-in-use remains appropriate.

In accordance with the provisions of IAS 36, a series of assumptions we use when calculating value in use include:
The second-term profit growth rate: 3% in each period after 2029 (2024: 3%), does not exceed the GDP growth rate forecast in mainland China, and is similar to the forecasts of external analysts.
The long-term asset growth rate: 3.25% in each period after 2029 (2024: 3.25%), which is the expected asset growth rate that will drive the long-term profit growth rate to 3%.
Discount rate: 8.08% (2024: 8.53%), calculated using market data and based on the capital asset pricing model. The discount rate used ranges from 7.1% to
8.7% (2024: 7.1% to 8.8%) and decreases mainly due to a market-led decline in risk-free rates.

Expected credit losses as a percentage of customer loans: ranges from 0.67% to 0.87% (2024: 0.74% to 0.93%) in the short to medium term, reflecting the reported baseline credit performance of Mainland China. in 2029

The ratio for each period after the year is 0.87% (2024: 0.97%), reflecting the expectation that Bank of Communications' average expected credit losses as a percentage of customer loans in recent years will continue to be low.

Risk-weighted assets as a percentage of total assets: between 62% and 64.2% in the short to medium term (2024: 62% to 62.5%), reflecting that risk weighting will increase in the short term and is expected to increase subsequently.
Return to historical levels in recent years. The ratio for each period after 2029 is 62% (2024: 62%), which continues to be similar to Bank of Communications' actual performance in recent years.

Credit loss forecasts for the period have declined.

Operating income growth rate: ranging from 0.5% to 7.4% in the short to medium term (2024: 0.1% to 9.9%), which is similar to Bank of Communications' actual performance in recent years. Short- and medium-term net interest income forecasts decline, reflecting
This reflects the expected pressure on the net interest margin compared with the previous period, resulting in a decrease in net value in use.

The effective tax rate for the second period: 15% in each period after 2029 (2024: 15%), which is higher than the recent historical average and consistent with the OECD/G20 tax base erosion and profit shifting package
The recommended minimum tax rate for capacitive architectures is consistent.

At the end of each reporting period, HSBC Holdings reviews its investments in subsidiaries to identify indicators of impairment. When the carrying amount of an investment is greater than its recoverable amount, impairment is recognized. According to IAS 36,

The recoverable amount is the higher of the investment's fair value less costs of disposal and its value in use. Value in use is calculated by discounting management's cash flow forecasts for the investment. Cash flow refers to the subsidiary company's
free cash flow subject to the company's binding capital requirements.

Brazil’s Social Integration Tax and Social Security Contribution Tax Matters

Beginning in the late 1990s, HSBC Bank Brasil S.A. – Banco Múltiplo (“HSBC Brasil”) and other financial services institutions filed legal proceedings in Brazil challenging the social integration tax and social

Will safeguard the assessment of contribution taxes, which are federal taxes levied on the gross income of Brazilian legal entities. Brazil's Supreme Court selects three lawsuits to set standards to be applied in all related litigation,

One of the cases involved an insurance company (in 2007), and two cases involved other banks (in 2011). In June 2023, the court ruled that the financial services institution lost all three lawsuits. The court is ruling

The standards established at the time of the decision have not yet been applied to HSBC Brazil’s existing litigation. After HSBC’s business in Brazil was sold to Bradesco in 2016, the relevant liabilities were still borne by HSBC. May 2025,

The court of first instance issued a favorable judgment in Brazil's HSBC's second largest case related to social integration tax and social security contribution, and the Brazilian tax office has appealed. Various related lawsuits may arise

Different results, and any financial impact on HSBC, may depend on a number of factors. Based on available information, the Group has confirmed preparations for an existing lawsuit. All existing social integration taxes and
The remaining additional tax liability from the Social Security contribution litigation is up to $400 million. As of December 31, 2025, there was no provision for this amount.
Madoff Securities
In a fraud case related to Madoff Securities in Luxembourg in 2009, HSBC Securities Services Luxembourg (“HSSL”) sought legal advice from Herald Fund SPC (“Herald”).

Defend against claims seeking the return of securities and cash of US$521 million (plus interest) or damages of US$5.6 billion (plus interest). On October 24, 2025, the Luxembourg Supreme Court rejected

Returned HSSL's appeal against the Herald's claim for the return of securities, but admitted HSSL's appeal against the Herald's claim for the return of cash. HSSL will now lodge a second appeal with the Luxembourg Court of Appeal.

If HSSL fails in its second appeal, the amount to be paid by HSSL will be challenged in subsequent Court of Appeal proceedings. We have confirmed $1.1 billion in preparedness in response to developments. In view of the second time
The lawsuit is still pending, and the determination of the reimbursement amount involved is complex and uncertain, and the final financial impact may be significantly different.

Additional Tier 1 Capital – Contingent Convertible Securities

HSBC Holdings continues to issue contingent convertible securities and are included in the capital base as Additional Tier 1 capital securities that are fully compliant with Capital Regulation 2. These securities are mainly sold in the market and subsequently allotted to enterprises

Investors and fund managers. The net proceeds from the issuance will generally be used for HSBC Holdings’ general corporate purposes and to maintain or further enhance its capital base in compliance with Capital Regulation 2. Such

Securities bear interest at a fixed rate until their first reset date (unless previously redeemed in accordance with the relevant terms). If not redeemed, the securities will bear interest at a fixed rate on each reset date for subsequent five-year periods.

Interest calculation is equal to the sum of the reference interest rate applicable at the time of reset and the credit spread determined at the time of issuance. Interest on contingent convertible securities will only be payable when due at HSBC Holdings' sole discretion, and HSBC Holdings

Dividends in 2026

We maintain our policy of targeting a dividend payout ratio of 50% of earnings per common share in 2026, 2027 and 2028, subject to capital requirements. Accordingly, earnings per common share will continue to exclude significant
items that require attention and related impacts.

In fiscal year 2025, we will pay dividends in accordance with our dividend policy. Excluding significant noteworthy items and related impacts, we have achieved a dividend payout ratio of 50% of earnings per ordinary share.

Significant items requiring attention in 2025 mainly involve the impact on the income statement caused by the withdrawal or termination of non-strategic businesses. These items also include the dilution loss on our investment in Bank of Communications and the recognition of

Impairments, legal preparations related to the Madoff securities fraud, and proceeds from transactions completed during the previous reporting period, including the sale of the French retail banking business, the Canadian banking business, and the Argentina business.
impact on life.

The dividend policy adopted by the Board of Directors is designed to provide sustainable cash dividends while retaining flexible arrangements for future investment and business growth, supplemented by further shareholder distributions if appropriate.
Approved dividends are declared in US dollars, and shareholders can choose to receive cash dividends in US dollars, British pounds or Hong Kong dollars, or a combination of these three currencies.

HSBC continues to issue contingent convertible securities that are included in its capital base as additional Tier 1 capital securities that are fully compliant with Capital Regulation 2. For details of these securities, please see note 32 to the financial statements.

HSBC Holdings issued US$1.5 billion of 6.95% perpetual subordinated contingent convertible securities on February 27, 2025, and issued S$800 million of 5% perpetual subordinated contingent convertible securities on March 24, 2025, and
Issued US$2 billion 7.05% perpetual subordinated contingent convertible securities on June 5, 2025.
